channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Debunking the science used to push for prohibition | #GFN26 Workshop
item.description: Bad evidence can become powerful when it is used to justify prohibition. Studies with weak methods, selective comparisons or inflated claims can shape policy long after their limitations are forgotten. That matters in nicotine regulation, where fear-based science can make lower-risk products look more dangerous than they are and keep smokers away from switching. Public health should challenge poor evidence before it becomes law. (Published on July 21, 2026bygfn.tv)

item.title: What’s Behind the UK’s Migration to Nicotine Pouches?
item.description: Migration changes habits, routines and access to support — including the way people use nicotine. For some adults, nicotine pouches may offer a discreet, smoke-free option in new social, legal or workplace environments where smoking is harder to sustain. That does not make them risk-free, but it does show why real-life context matters. Harm reduction policy should understand how people actually adapt, not assume nicotine use happens in a vacuum. (Published on July 21, 2026byfiltermag.org)

item.title: Philippine tobacco policy illustrates limits of tax hikes, economist says
item.description: Tax hikes can only go so far if they ignore how people and markets respond. The Philippines’ experience shows that making cigarettes more expensive may reduce use for some, but it can also fuel illicit trade when legal products become unaffordable. Tobacco policy needs more than higher taxes; it needs enforcement, consumer insight and lower-risk alternatives that give smokers a realistic way out. A strategy that prices people into the black market is not the same as reducing harm. (Published on July 21, 2026bymanilastandard.net)

item.title: What South Korea Can Teach Europe About Taxing Nicotine
item.description: South Korea's high taxes on nicotine pouches, compared to cigarettes, hinder tobacco harm reduction efforts. In contrast, Sweden's lower taxes on smoke-free alternatives have drastically reduced smoking rates. South Korea's stringent regulations treat all nicotine products like cigarettes, impeding public health goals. Korea's lack of progress highlights the importance of distinguishing between products based on harm. For the EU, decisions on taxation and regulation will impact tobacco control efforts. Following Sweden's approach of targeting harm reduction over consumption could lead to lower smoking rates and healthcare costs. The EU should consider Sweden's model when updating tobacco directives to prioritize public health. (Published on July 21, 2026byworldvapersalliance.com)
